text:
The chapter reviews very rapidly developing changes in the higher education systems in the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) countries, revealing the dynamics and tension between rapid growth and institutional readiness. Over the last two decades, GCC governments achieved remarkable increases in access to higher education, mainly through the introduction of new forms of partnerships between the public and private sectors, and by spreading the new trend of developing Western-style universities in the GCC countries, and achieving thereby an educational revolution, which enrolled in particular women and the children of expatriate workers in higher education and hence further empowering women in particular. The educational revolution which took place in the higher education sector of the GCC countries came at a time when the countries of the Council are endeavoring in their efforts and developmental plans to fulfill the aims and objectives of modernization on one hand and to keep pace with its impact on maintaining Arab-Islamic civiliations and values on the other, thus encountering the challenges resulting from the unprecedented, speed, impact and nature of the educational revolution.

English language teaching in the Saudi universities The implementation of English language teaching and the use of Western administrative systems in the universities is seen in policy statements and curricula as an easy way of linking Saudi Arabia with the global knowledge economy. Thus, English and Western administrative practices have to be marginalized, and the role of the Arabic language and of Islamic culture have to be minimized in order to secure foreign investment and credits. Furthermore, the reforms to higher education seem to proceed independently of the socio-economic development of the countries in the region. Graduation rates have soared but nationalization of the labour market continues to be unsuccessful due to private sector incentives being misaligned with social goals and due to wages in the public sector remaining low. Women empowerment through the education sector has also been another dimension of the higher education reforms in the region, but has been often hampered by the persistence of a number of cultural and economic barriers for women full employment in the world of work. For all these developments to be sustained and for the expected benefits of higher education to trickle down and be widely available for the different social and economic groups in the region, higher education has to move from being seen as an entrepreneurial and utilitarian 'turnkey project' into being one that that enhances not only skills but learning that is critically orientated and socially meaningful in a context which is full of awareness of the rich historical, cultural and social context of the Middle East region.
